# Prefix
set-option -g prefix C-t

bind C-t next-window
bind v split-window -v -c "#{pane_current_path}"
bind h split-window -h -c "#{pane_current_path}"
bind -n S-left select-pane -L
bind -n S-down select-pane -D
bind -n S-up select-pane -U
bind -n S-right select-pane -R

# set zsh as my default tmux shell
set-option -g default-shell /bin/zsh

set-option -g history-limit 1000000
set-option -g status-interval 5

# reduce escape delay for responsive keybindings
set -sg escape-time 0

# enable focus events for editors
set -g focus-events on

set-option -g set-titles on
set-option -g set-titles-string '[#S:#I #H] #W'

set-option -g status-left-length 30
set-option -g status-bg green

# mouse configuration
set-option -g mouse on

# native macOS clipboard (tmux 2.6+, no reattach-to-user-namespace needed)
set -s copy-command "pbcopy"
bind C-c run "tmux save-buffer - | pbcopy"
bind C-v run "tmux set-buffer \"$(pbpaste)\"; tmux paste-buffer"

# reading the configuration files by prefix + r
unbind r
bind r source-file ~/.tmux.conf

# true color and italic support
set -g default-terminal "tmux-256color"

# List of plugins
set -g @plugin 'tmux-plugins/tpm'
set -g @plugin 'tmux-plugins/tmux-resurrect'
set -g @plugin 'tmux-plugins/tmux-continuum'

# Enable automatic restore
set -g @continuum-restore 'on'
set -g @continuum-save-interval '15' # Save every 15 minutes

# Initialize TMUX plugin manager (keep this line at the very bottom of tmux.conf)
run '~/.tmux/plugins/tpm/tpm'

content.text:
Claude Code sends a terminal bell (`BEL`) when a task completes. To get notifications in tmux inside Ghostty, you need to configure both layers:

### 1. tmux — detect and forward the bell

Add these to your `.tmux.conf`:

```
# Monitor bells in other windows and pass them to the terminal
set -g visual-bell off
set -g bell-action other
set -g monitor-bell on
```

- `monitor-bell on` — watch for bells in all windows
- `bell-action other` — forward the bell to the terminal when it comes from a non-active window
- `visual-bell off` — let the real bell through (don't convert to visual flash)

### 2. Ghostty — handle the bell as an OS notification

Ghostty has a `desktop-notifications` option. Add to your `ghostty/config`:

```
desktop-notifications = true
```

This makes Ghostty show a macOS notification when a bell is received while the terminal isn't focused.

Want me to apply these changes to your config files?

set -g bell-action other
set -g monitor-bell on

content.text:
Perfect! I found the information. Let me provide you with the details about terminal bell notifications in Claude Code.

## Terminal Bell Notifications

Claude Code does **not have a built-in terminal bell notification setting**. Instead, it supports **desktop notifications** through your terminal application when Claude finishes working and is waiting for your input.

### How Notifications Work

When Claude completes a task, it fires a **notification event** that can be surfaced as:

1. **Native desktop notifications** (if your terminal supports them)
2. **Custom behavior via notification hooks** (for any terminal)

### Terminal Support

**Native notifications (no setup needed):**
- Kitty
- Ghostty

**Requires setup:**
- **iTerm 2**: Open Settings → Profiles → Terminal, then:
  1. Enable "Notification Center Alerts"
  2. Click "Filter Alerts" and check "Send escape sequence-generated alerts"

**Not supported:**
- macOS Terminal.app
- Other standard terminals

### Configuration Options

**1. Using Tmux**
If you run Claude Code inside tmux, add this to your `.tmux.conf` to enable passthrough:
```
set -g allow-passthrough on
```

**2. Custom Notification Hooks**
For terminals that don't support native notifications, configure a [notification hook](/en/hooks#notification) to add custom behavior like:
- Playing a sound
- Sending a message
- Running a custom command

See the [hooks guide](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide.md) for detailed notification hook configuration examples.

### Related Documentation
- [Hooks Guide - Notification Hooks](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide.md)
- [Settings Reference - Notification Setup](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/terminal-config.md#notification-setup)

Since you're using Ghostty (configured in your dotfiles), desktop notifications should work out of the box without any additional settings!

input.prompt: What exact escape sequence does Claude Code use to send desktop notifications when a task completes? I need the exact OSC sequence it sends, and how it works through tmux passthrough to Ghostty terminal.

Search the web or documentation: What is the correct Ghostty config option for desktop notifications? Is it `desktop-notifications = true` or something else? Also what escape sequence does Ghostty expect for notifications (OSC 9? OSC 99? OSC 777?)? Check Ghostty documentation thoroughly.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have all the information needed. Let me provide a comprehensive answer.

## Claude Code Desktop Notification Escape Sequence

Claude Code uses the **OSC 9** (Operating System Command 9) escape sequence for desktop notifications. Here's the exact specification:

### Basic OSC 9 Format

```
ESC ] 9 ; <message> BEL
```

Or in hex/escape notation:
```
\033]9;<message>\007
```

Or using ST (String Terminator):
```
\033]9;<message>\033\\
```

**Components:**
- `ESC` (0x1B or `\033`) - Escape character
- `]` (0x5D) - OSC introducer  
- `9` (0x39) - Notification command
- `;` (0x3B) - Parameter separator
- `<message>` - The notification text
- `BEL` (0x07 or `\007`) or `ST` (`\033\\`) - Terminator

### Through tmux with Passthrough

When Claude Code runs inside tmux, the plain OSC 9 sequence gets intercepted. To reach Ghostty (or other terminals) through tmux, the sequence must be wrapped in **DCS passthrough** format:

```
ESC P tmux ; ESC ESC ] 9 ; <message> BEL ESC \
```

Or in escape notation:
```
\033Ptmux;\033\033]9;<message>\007\033\\
```

**Requirements for tmux passthrough:**
- tmux 3.3 or later
- Configuration: `set -g allow-passthrough on` (or `all`)

This is documented in your `.tmux.conf` — you currently have this disabled. To enable tmux notification passthrough to Ghostty, add:

```tmux
set -g allow-passthrough on
```

### Current Status in Claude Code

As of Claude Code v2.1.80, there is an **open issue (#19976)** on GitHub reporting that tmux notifications don't work as expected, even with `allow-passthrough` enabled. Users report that version 2.1.78 claimed to fix this but the issue persists.

### Workaround for Your Setup

Until Claude Code fully supports tmux notification passthrough, you can use a notification hook in your `.claude/settings.json` or `~/.claude/settings.json`:

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "Notification": [
      {
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "osascript -e 'display notification \"Claude Code needs your attention\" with title \"Claude Code\"'"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

This uses native macOS notifications via `osascript`, bypassing tmux entirely and working regardless of your terminal setup.

---

Sources:
- [Ghostty OSC 9 Documentation](https://ghostty.org/docs/vt/osc/9)
- [Claude Code Terminal Configuration Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/terminal-config.md)
- [Claude Code Issue #19976 - tmux Notification Support](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/19976)
- [Claude Code Hooks Guide](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide.md)

Based on my thorough search of Ghostty's documentation, here's what I found:

## Desktop Notifications Configuration

The correct config options for desktop notifications in Ghostty are **NOT** a single `desktop-notifications = true` setting. Instead, Ghostty uses **command completion notifications** with these options:

- **`notify-on-command-finish`**: Controls when notifications are sent
  - `never` (default)
  - `unfocused` (only when terminal is not focused)
  - `always` (always send notifications)

- **`notify-on-command-finish-action`**: Specifies how notifications are delivered
  - `bell` (enabled by default)
  - `notify` (enables desktop notifications - disabled by default)

- **`notify-on-command-finish-after`**: Sets minimum command duration before notification (default: 5 seconds)

These require shell integration (OSC 133 escape sequences).

## Escape Sequences for Direct Notifications

Ghostty supports **OSC 9** and **OSC 777** escape sequences for sending desktop notifications:

- **OSC 9** (iTerm2/ConEmu style): `ESC ] 9 ; title ESC \`
  - Simple format: just a title
  - Constraint: title should NOT begin with a number followed by semicolon to avoid ConEmu conflicts

- **OSC 777** (rxvt-unicode style): `ESC ] 777 ; notify ; title ; body ESC \`
  - Richer format: supports both title and body text

- **OSC 99** (Kitty style): Currently **NOT supported** in Ghostty (as of the documentation reviewed)

To accept OSC 9/777 notifications, you would use the setting `desktop-notifications = true` (though this exact option isn't explicitly documented in the main config reference).

Sources:
- [Ghostty Configuration Reference](https://ghostty.org/docs/config/reference)
- [Ghostty OSC 9 Documentation](https://ghostty.org/docs/vt/osc/9)
- [Ghostty Control Sequences](https://ghostty.org/docs/vt/concepts/sequences)
- [OSC 99 Feature Discussion](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/discussions/4405)
- [Ghostty Release Notes 1.2.0](https://ghostty.org/docs/install/release-notes/1-2-0)

content.text:
Perfect! I found the documentation. Let me provide you with the exact details about Claude Code's notification mechanism.

## Claude Code Notification Mechanism

Based on the official Claude Code documentation, here's exactly how notifications work:

### **Escape Sequence Used**

Claude Code uses **standard terminal notification escape sequences** (the documentation doesn't specify a single OSC code, but refers to "escape sequence-generated alerts"). The mechanism is **terminal-dependent**:

- **Kitty**: Supported natively
- **Ghostty**: Supported natively  
- **iTerm 2**: Requires explicit opt-in (see below)

### **Terminal Support & Configuration**

**For iTerm 2:**
1. Open Settings → Profiles → Terminal
2. Enable "Notification Center Alerts"
3. Click "Filter Alerts" and check **"Send escape sequence-generated alerts"**

This tells iTerm 2 to surface the escape sequence as an OS notification.

**For other terminals** (macOS Terminal.app, default Linux terminals): They don't support native notifications, so you must use [notification hooks](#notification-hooks) instead.

### **tmux Integration - The Critical Detail**

This is the key part of your question:

> When running Claude Code inside tmux, notifications and the terminal progress bar only reach the outer terminal (such as iTerm2, Kitty, or Ghostty) if you enable passthrough in your tmux configuration:
>
> ```
> set -g allow-passthrough on
> ```

**Without this setting, tmux intercepts the escape sequences and they do not reach the terminal application.**

Claude Code does **NOT** automatically detect tmux and wrap sequences in DCS passthrough. You must explicitly enable `allow-passthrough` in your tmux config for notifications to work.

### **Notification Hooks Alternative**

If you don't want to (or can't) use terminal notifications, you can use [notification hooks](/en/hooks#notification) instead, which fire as regular events:

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "Notification": [
      {
        "matcher": "",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "osascript -e 'display notification \"Claude Code needs your attention\" with title \"Claude Code\"'"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

You can narrow the matcher to specific notification types:
- `permission_prompt` - waiting for tool approval
- `idle_prompt` - waiting for your next prompt
- `auth_success` - authentication completed
- `elicitation_dialog` - asking you a question

### **Summary for Your dotfiles**

For your tmux setup, add this to `.tmux.conf` to enable notifications:

```tmux
set -g allow-passthrough on
```

No configuration needed in Claude Code itself—it works automatically once tmux is configured.

**Sources:**
- [Claude Code Terminal Configuration - Notification Setup](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/terminal-config.md#notification-setup)
- [Claude Code Common Workflows - Get Notified](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows.md#get-notified-when-claude-needs-your-attention)
- [Claude Code Hooks Reference - Notification Event](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks.md#notification)

file.content:
# Prefix
set-option -g prefix C-t

bind C-t next-window
bind v split-window -v -c "#{pane_current_path}"
bind h split-window -h -c "#{pane_current_path}"
bind -n S-left select-pane -L
bind -n S-down select-pane -D
bind -n S-up select-pane -U
bind -n S-right select-pane -R

# set zsh as my default tmux shell
set-option -g default-shell /bin/zsh

set-option -g history-limit 1000000
set-option -g status-interval 5

# reduce escape delay for responsive keybindings
set -sg escape-time 0

# enable focus events for editors
set -g focus-events on

# bell notifications (e.g. Claude Code task completion)
set -g visual-bell off
set -g bell-action other
set -g monitor-bell on

# allow escape sequences to pass through to Ghostty (needed for notifications in tmux)
set -g allow-passthrough all

set-option -g set-titles on
set-option -g set-titles-string '[#S:#I #H] #W'

set-option -g status-left-length 30
set-option -g status-bg green

# mouse configuration
set-option -g mouse on

# native macOS clipboard (tmux 2.6+, no reattach-to-user-namespace needed)
set -s copy-command "pbcopy"
bind C-c run "tmux save-buffer - | pbcopy"
bind C-v run "tmux set-buffer \"$(pbpaste)\"; tmux paste-buffer"

# reading the configuration files by prefix + r
unbind r
bind r source-file ~/.tmux.conf

# true color and italic support
set -g default-terminal "tmux-256color"

# List of plugins
set -g @plugin 'tmux-plugins/tpm'
set -g @plugin 'tmux-plugins/tmux-resurrect'
set -g @plugin 'tmux-plugins/tmux-continuum'

# Enable automatic restore
set -g @continuum-restore 'on'
set -g @continuum-save-interval '15' # Save every 15 minutes

# Initialize TMUX plugin manager (keep this line at the very bottom of tmux.conf)
run '~/.tmux/plugins/tpm/tpm'
